question: What first drew you to archaeology?
lookingFor: A genuine, specific origin story — not a generic love of history, but a moment or experience that sparked real interest in the discipline.

bullets: Name a specific moment, book, site visit, or experience — not a vague childhood interest
Connect it to what archaeology actually does (material culture, the human past) rather than just 'history'
Show that interest has grown since that first spark
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: It started when my family visited a Roman villa site and I watched a conservator brushing soil away from a mosaic floor. I remember thinking that someone had stood on that floor nearly two thousand years ago, and here we were seeing it for the first time in centuries — that felt extraordinary to me. I went home and read everything I could about how archaeologists actually reconstruct daily life from fragments, and that question — how do you turn objects into stories? — is still what drives me.

commonMistakes: Saying 'I've always loved history' with nothing more specific
Conflating archaeology with history or classics without distinguishing the material focus
No evidence of sustained interest beyond the initial spark
followUps: How has your understanding of what archaeology involves changed since then?

question: Why do you want to study archaeology here specifically?
lookingFor: Evidence of real research into the department — a specific module, fieldschool, staff research area, or course feature tied to a personal goal.

bullets: Name something concrete and specific to this department — not just 'great reputation'
Connect it to your own interests or where you want to specialise
Show you understand what makes this course different from alternatives
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I was drawn to your department partly because of the integrated fieldschool in the second year — having guaranteed excavation experience built into the degree matters a lot to me because I learn by doing. I also read about Professor Hartley's work on urban Roman archaeology, which sits right at the intersection of field methods and social interpretation that I'm most interested in. A lot of courses treat those as separate tracks; yours seems to hold them together, and that's exactly where I want to develop.

commonMistakes: Generic praise about reputation or prestige with no specifics
Mentioning a module or staff member that belongs to a different institution
Framing it entirely around the city or social life rather than the academic offering
followUps: What would you bring to the department that we'd notice?

question: Tell us about a book, article, or documentary on archaeology that you've engaged with recently. What did you take from it?
lookingFor: Evidence of reading or viewing beyond the school curriculum, with genuine critical engagement rather than plot summary.

bullets: Name something specific — a title, author, or publication — not 'various things'
Say what the argument or finding was, not just the topic
Add your own reaction: what it confirmed, challenged, or made you want to explore further
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I read Richard Bradley's The Idea of Order recently, which argues that prehistoric monument builders had a more coherent understanding of landscape and memory than we often credit them with. What struck me was how Bradley uses the positioning of monuments relative to earlier sites to make that argument — he's reading intention from spatial relationships, not texts. It made me think harder about what counts as evidence of abstract thought in the archaeological record, which I hadn't considered as a methodological question before.

commonMistakes: Summarising the book without offering any personal reaction or critique
Choosing something too popular or superficial and failing to engage with its argument
Picking something they clearly haven't read and being unable to go into detail
followUps: Did anything in it challenge a view you already held?

question: Have you had any hands-on experience in archaeology — excavation, fieldwalking, museum work, anything like that?
lookingFor: Real engagement with the practical side of the discipline, and the ability to reflect on what that experience taught them about how archaeology works.

bullets: Describe what you did concretely — where, what type of work, what the context was
Reflect on what you learned about methodology or the discipline from it
If experience is limited, show you've sought it out and know what fieldwork involves
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I spent a week on a community excavation at a medieval farmstead site last summer — mostly mattocking and sieving, but I also got to learn how to draw a section and record finds properly. What surprised me was how much interpretation happens in the trench, moment to moment, rather than just in the lab afterwards. The site director showed us how the same layer could look very different across a two-metre stretch, and that decisions made on the ground shape what you can claim later. It made me much more interested in field methodology as an intellectual problem, not just a technique.

commonMistakes: Only mentioning a museum visit or school trip without any reflection
Describing experience in vague terms with no detail about what the work involved
Treating fieldwork as purely physical and missing its interpretive dimension
followUps: What aspect of fieldwork do you think you'd find most challenging, and why?

question: How would you explain the difference between archaeology and history to someone who thought they were basically the same thing?
lookingFor: A clear disciplinary distinction — material culture vs. written record — and some appreciation of what archaeology can access that history cannot.

bullets: Anchor the difference in evidence: archaeology works from material culture, history primarily from texts
Note what archaeology can reach that history often can't — pre-literate societies, ordinary lives, long timescales
Show awareness that the disciplines overlap and sometimes collaborate
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: History relies mainly on written records, which means it can only really reach literate societies and the people who left documents — usually the powerful. Archaeology works from physical remains: objects, buildings, landscapes, even DNA — which means it can tell us about daily life across all social levels, and about periods and places with no writing at all. That's a huge range of human experience that history alone can't access. They overlap — medieval archaeologists read documents alongside their finds — but they start from fundamentally different kinds of evidence, and that shapes the questions each discipline can ask.

commonMistakes: Saying archaeology is 'just older history' without recognising the methodological difference
Being unable to say anything about what archaeology uniquely contributes
Ignoring the collaborative relationship between the two disciplines
followUps: Is there a question you think archaeology is better placed to answer than history? Give me an example.

question: Is there a particular period or region of archaeology that excites you most, and what is it about that area that draws you in?
lookingFor: A genuine area of passion backed by some knowledge — not a vague answer like 'ancient Egypt because it's impressive', but a specific hook into the questions that period raises.

bullets: Name a specific period or region and something substantive about it — not just the famous sites
Explain what intellectual question or puzzle draws you there
Show openness to learning across the whole discipline even while having a current focus
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I've been most drawn to the Iron Age of Atlantic Europe — the period before and during Roman contact, when societies that left no writing of their own were interacting with a world that did. I find it a fascinating methodological problem: you're trying to understand communities entirely on their own terms through material culture, and then work out what really changed when Rome arrived versus what was already transforming. It also feels politically alive to me — questions about who gets to tell a society's story when they didn't write it down seem very relevant beyond archaeology.

commonMistakes: Naming Egypt or Rome purely because of popular culture with no substance behind it
Being so general that it reveals no actual knowledge of the period
Suggesting they only care about one period and showing no curiosity about the wider discipline
followUps: What's one open question in that period that you'd like to see resolved, and how might you approach it?

question: Can you explain how radiocarbon dating works and what its limitations are?
lookingFor: A clear, correct explanation of the principle — not a textbook recitation — plus genuine engagement with its limits, showing scientific thinking.

bullets: Explain the core principle: carbon-14 is taken up by living organisms and decays at a known rate after death
Mention calibration and why it matters
Name at least two real limitations — age range, contamination, the plateau problem, or material restrictions
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: All living things absorb carbon, including a small proportion of radioactive carbon-14, which decays at a known rate once an organism dies. By measuring how much C-14 remains in a sample, you can estimate when it died. The result has to be calibrated against a reference curve because the amount of C-14 in the atmosphere hasn't been constant over time. The main limitations are the age range — it's unreliable beyond about 50,000 years — and the requirement for organic material, so it can't date stone directly. Contamination is also a real risk; if newer carbon gets into the sample, you get a falsely recent date. I find the calibration problem particularly interesting because it means the same measurement can give you a broad date range in some periods and a tight one in others.

commonMistakes: Confusing carbon dating with other radiometric methods like potassium-argon
Stating it can date any material including stone or ceramics
Knowing the principle but being unaware of any limitations
followUps: Can you think of a scenario where a radiocarbon date might be misleading even if the lab did everything correctly?

question: What is stratigraphy and why does it matter in excavation?
lookingFor: Understanding of the law of superposition applied to archaeology, plus awareness of contexts where it can mislead.

bullets: Define stratigraphy: the study of layers and their sequence to establish relative chronology
Explain why sequence matters: what's below is generally older
Acknowledge complications — intrusion, truncation, bioturbation — showing critical awareness
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Stratigraphy is the study of how deposits have built up in sequence over time. The basic principle is that in undisturbed soil, what's lower is generally older — you're reading the layers like pages in a book, but from the bottom up. In excavation it gives you a relative chronology even without any absolute dates: you know context A was deposited before context B because B sits on top of A. The complications arise when the sequence has been disturbed — a later pit dug through earlier layers, animal burrowing, or ploughing can mix material and make older finds appear in younger contexts. Understanding those disturbances is as important as reading the sequence itself.

commonMistakes: Defining stratigraphy as only about rock layers (confusing it with geology without the archaeological application)
Stating that lower always means older without acknowledging exceptions
Not knowing what a context or a section drawing is
followUps: How would you approach a context where the stratigraphy seemed contradicted by the finds?

question: Should archaeologists always excavate when they have the opportunity, or are there cases where they should leave a site undisturbed?
lookingFor: Ethical reasoning that balances knowledge generation against preservation, community rights, and the irreversibility of excavation.

bullets: Acknowledge that excavation is destructive and irreversible — a key ethical constraint
Raise community or descendant-community rights, especially for burial sites
Argue for a proportionate, question-led approach rather than 'dig everything'
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Excavation is destruction — you only get one chance to read the stratigraphy, and once it's gone it's gone. That means there has to be a good reason to dig: a research question that requires it, a development threat that makes preservation in situ impossible, or a genuine knowledge gap. Burial sites raise a harder question, especially where descendant communities have strong claims on how their ancestors are treated — NAGPRA in the US is one legal framework that reflects that, and I think the principle behind it is right regardless of jurisdiction. My instinct is that the default should be preservation, with excavation as the justified exception rather than the default.

commonMistakes: Arguing that knowledge is always the priority without acknowledging community rights
Ignoring the irreversibility of excavation
Giving a vague answer that doesn't commit to any position
followUps: How would you handle a situation where a community wanted a site left alone but you believed it was at risk of destruction?

question: If you found an unusual object on an excavation and had no idea what it was, how would you go about trying to interpret it?
lookingFor: A systematic, evidence-led approach to interpretation — context, comparanda, specialist consultation — rather than guessing or over-claiming.

bullets: Start with the context: where was it found, with what, in what layer?
Consider form, material, and manufacture traces as evidence of function
Seek comparanda from published finds and consult specialists — don't over-interpret alone
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The first thing I'd do is resist the temptation to guess, because the object itself is only part of the picture. Context is crucial — what else was in the same layer, was it near a structure, was it a deliberate deposit or rubbish? Then I'd describe it systematically: material, dimensions, any traces of manufacture or use-wear, any residues. That description lets you search for parallels in the literature, which is often where interpretation starts. I'd also flag it for specialist analysis — a finds specialist or, if appropriate, a materials scientist — because my interpretation is always provisional until it's been tested against broader knowledge.

commonMistakes: Jumping straight to an interpretation without discussing methodology
Treating the object in isolation from its context
Presenting a single interpretation as certain rather than provisional
followUps: What if the context had been disturbed and you had almost no reliable provenance?

question: Archaeology is increasingly described as a science. Do you agree, and what does that mean for how the discipline works?
lookingFor: Nuanced thinking about what 'science' means in this context — hypothesis testing, new analytical techniques — alongside recognition of archaeology's interpretive and humanistic dimensions.

bullets: Acknowledge the genuine scientific methods now central to archaeology — aDNA, isotope analysis, LiDAR, etc.
Note that interpretation of meaning and social context retains a humanistic dimension
Argue for archaeology as an interdisciplinary field rather than settling the 'science vs humanities' binary
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd say it's both, and the binary is part of the problem. Modern archaeology uses rigorous scientific methods — ancient DNA analysis, stable isotope studies to trace diet and mobility, ground-penetrating radar, LiDAR to find sites under forest cover — and those involve hypothesis testing, statistics, and peer review in a fully scientific sense. But interpreting what a set of burial practices means socially, or how identity was expressed through material culture, requires a humanistic kind of reasoning that doesn't reduce to numbers. I think the most interesting archaeology right now happens at the junction, and being comfortable in both registers is part of what makes it such a rich discipline.

commonMistakes: Claiming archaeology is simply a natural science without acknowledging interpretive work
Claiming it's purely a humanities subject and ignoring the scientific methods now central to it
Being unable to name any specific scientific technique used in modern archaeology
followUps: Which scientific technique do you think has most transformed archaeology in the last twenty years?

question: Ancient DNA research has transformed our understanding of prehistoric population movements. What excites you about it, and what concerns might it raise?
lookingFor: Genuine engagement with the science and its findings, alongside ethical and methodological awareness — not just enthusiasm without critical thinking.

bullets: Name something specific aDNA has revealed — migration, admixture, disease — showing real knowledge
Acknowledge the methodological challenges: contamination, sample bias, representativeness
Raise at least one ethical concern — indigenous data sovereignty, reductive identity claims
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: What excites me is the way aDNA has rewritten narratives we thought were settled — the evidence for large-scale migration into Europe during the Neolithic and Bronze Age, for example, showed that cultural change was often tied to population movement in ways the material culture alone couldn't prove. At the same time, there are real concerns. The samples are geographically and chronologically uneven, which means the picture we're building has significant gaps. There are also questions about whose ancestors are being studied and who controls that data — some Indigenous communities have pushed back strongly on research using remains from their ancestral populations, and I think those objections deserve serious engagement rather than dismissal.

commonMistakes: Treating aDNA as a straightforward answer machine without methodological caveats
Not knowing any specific finding from aDNA research
Ignoring the ethical and political dimensions around ancestral remains
followUps: How should archaeologists balance the scientific value of aDNA research against community objections to studying ancestral remains?

question: What is landscape archaeology and why might it tell us things that an individual excavation cannot?
lookingFor: Understanding of the shift from single-site to landscape-scale thinking, and what that perspective reveals about past human behaviour.

bullets: Define landscape archaeology: studying patterns across a wider area rather than individual sites in isolation
Explain what it reveals: land use, movement, settlement hierarchies, environmental relationships
Give an example of a technique — fieldwalking, remote sensing, GIS — that makes it possible
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Landscape archaeology treats the whole environment as the unit of study rather than focusing on a single site in isolation. A single excavation tells you a lot about one place at one time, but it can't tell you how that place related to others nearby, how people moved through the territory, or how land use changed across centuries. By combining fieldwalking, aerial photography, LiDAR, and GIS analysis, you can start to map settlement patterns, trackways, field systems, and boundaries across a whole region. Stonehenge is a good example — it only makes sense when you look at the wider ceremonial landscape around it, not the monument in isolation.

commonMistakes: Conflating landscape archaeology with simply doing lots of excavations across a region
Being unable to name any technique used in landscape survey
Ignoring the temporal dimension — landscape archaeology tracks change over time, not just space
followUps: What are the limitations of remote sensing methods like LiDAR compared to ground-level survey?

question: Should museums return objects collected during the colonial period to their countries of origin?
lookingFor: Structured ethical reasoning that engages with the real arguments on multiple sides — not a knee-jerk position — with some awareness of specific cases.

bullets: Acknowledge the genuine arguments on both sides rather than presenting it as simple
Engage with a specific case or principle — the Elgin Marbles, Benin Bronzes, or a repatriation framework
Commit to a reasoned position while acknowledging what you're trading off
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I find this genuinely difficult, which I think is the honest response. The case for repatriation is strong where objects were taken through force, theft, or during colonial dispossession — the Benin Bronzes being a clear example where the circumstances of acquisition were violent. The counter-arguments — universal access, conservation capacity, political instability — have some force but often feel like post-hoc rationalisations for keeping things rather than principled positions. My instinct is that the burden of proof should fall on the institution holding the object, not the claimant, and that each case needs to be assessed on its specific history rather than a blanket rule. Repatriation doesn't have to mean the objects disappear from view — loans, partnerships, and digital access can coexist with return.

commonMistakes: Taking a simple position without engaging with the counter-arguments
Being unable to name a specific case or agreement
Avoiding the question by saying 'it depends' without specifying on what
followUps: Does your answer change if the country of origin has a less stable government or fewer conservation resources?

question: What kinds of evidence can maritime or underwater archaeology provide that land archaeology often cannot?
lookingFor: Awareness that underwater sites can preserve organic materials exceptionally well, and understanding of what that reveals about trade, seafaring, and daily life.

bullets: Note the exceptional preservation conditions — anaerobic, cold, low-disturbance environments
Name the types of evidence preserved: wood, textiles, food remains, cargo assemblages
Give an example — a specific wreck or site — to show genuine engagement
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Underwater contexts can preserve organic materials that almost never survive on land — wood, rope, leather, food, sometimes even textiles — because the anaerobic conditions slow decay dramatically. A shipwreck like the Mary Rose or the Bronze Age wrecks at Uluburun gives you a snapshot of a moment: the cargo was sealed at the point of sinking, which means you can reconstruct trade routes, commercial relationships, and even personal possessions in ways that a land site, which accumulates over time, can't easily offer. It's also one of the few contexts where you might find an intact assemblage from a specific voyage, rather than a palimpsest of many episodes.

commonMistakes: Naming underwater archaeology as purely about shipwrecks and ignoring submerged landscapes or port sites
Not being able to name any specific site or wreck
Missing the preservation argument, which is the key insight
followUps: What specific challenges does underwater excavation pose compared to land-based fieldwork?

subtopic: Gender and identity in the past
question: How can archaeologists study gender in past societies, and what are the risks of those approaches?
lookingFor: Awareness of the methodological debate — osteological sex vs. social gender, grave goods interpretation — and the danger of projecting present-day assumptions onto the past.

bullets: Distinguish biological sex (osteological analysis) from social gender (inferred from material culture and context)
Name the main source of evidence — grave goods, spatial patterns, iconography — and their interpretive limitations
Raise the risk of anachronism: assuming past gender categories map onto ours
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Archaeologists approach gender mainly through two angles: osteological analysis of skeletal remains, which can indicate biological sex, and material culture — what someone was buried with, how they were positioned, what spaces they occupied. The problem is that we've often assumed a simple mapping between biological sex and social role, which doesn't hold even in many contemporary societies, let alone past ones. The famous Birka warrior burial, initially assumed male because of the grave goods, turned out osteologically female — and the debate about what to conclude from that is itself revealing. The bigger risk is assuming our own gender categories were universal, when the evidence might be pointing to something more complex that we don't have categories for yet.

commonMistakes: Treating osteological sex and social gender as equivalent without comment
Not acknowledging the interpretive risks or potential for anachronism
Being unaware of any specific case study or debate in the literature
followUps: How should an archaeologist report a finding about gender if the evidence is genuinely ambiguous?

question: What do you think archaeologists owe to the communities whose past they are studying?
lookingFor: Engagement with the ethical and relational dimensions of archaeology — not just data extraction — including co-production, transparency, and genuine benefit to communities.

bullets: Move beyond 'publishing results' to active communication and co-production with communities
Acknowledge that 'the community' can mean multiple overlapping groups with different interests
Show awareness of examples where community archaeology has worked well
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I think the baseline obligation is transparency — not just publishing in academic journals, but communicating results in ways communities can actually access and engage with. But I think the real shift in recent decades is toward co-production: involving local communities not just as audiences but as active participants in setting research questions, interpreting finds, and deciding what happens to material. That's not just ethically right — it also tends to produce better archaeology, because local knowledge is real knowledge. It gets complicated when 'the community' is internally divided, or when different groups have competing claims on the same past, and those tensions are something the discipline is still working through.

commonMistakes: Treating community engagement as purely a PR exercise
Assuming 'the community' is a single unified group with one view
Limiting obligations to academic publication without thinking about access and communication
followUps: Can you think of a case where community involvement actually changed what questions were asked or how findings were interpreted?

question: What modern technologies have changed how archaeologists find and record sites, and which do you find most interesting?
lookingFor: Awareness of at least two or three specific technologies — LiDAR, GPR, photogrammetry, drone survey — and some substantive reason for finding one of them compelling.

bullets: Name at least two specific technologies with a sentence on what each does
Explain what they enable that wasn't possible before
Offer a genuine reason for being particularly interested in one of them
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: LiDAR has been transformative — it strips away vegetation digitally to reveal the ground surface, which has led to huge discoveries in forested areas like the Maya lowlands where sites were essentially invisible before. Ground-penetrating radar lets you see below the surface without digging, which is valuable both for research and for preservation decisions. Photogrammetry with drones means you can produce incredibly detailed 3D models of a site in a day, which transforms recording and means researchers who weren't there can interrogate the data later. The one I find most interesting is LiDAR, because it's literally showing us a different world that was always there and we couldn't see — the scale of what's been found in the last decade is extraordinary.

commonMistakes: Only being able to name one technology vaguely
Confusing what different tools actually do
Showing no genuine interest — treating it as a list to recite
followUps: What limitations do these remote sensing methods have that mean old-fashioned fieldwalking is still worth doing?

question: Archaeologists sometimes say that all interpretation is shaped by the interpreter's own background and assumptions. Do you agree, and does it matter?
lookingFor: Engagement with the philosophy of archaeological interpretation — the reflexivity debate — with the ability to hold the insight without collapsing into relativism.

bullets: Agree that interpretation is never neutral, with a concrete example of how bias has shaped past archaeology
Argue that acknowledging bias is not the same as saying all interpretations are equally valid
Suggest what reflexivity in practice looks like — being explicit about assumptions, peer review, diverse teams
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I do agree, and I think the history of the discipline shows it clearly. For much of the twentieth century, archaeologists read burial evidence through very gendered lenses that said more about their own assumptions than the past. The processualist response was to demand more rigorous methods, but post-processualists were right to argue that you can't fully remove the interpreter from the interpretation. I don't think that means all interpretations are equally valid — some are better evidenced, better argued, and more consistent with the data than others. What it means in practice is being explicit about your assumptions, inviting critique, and building more diverse research teams who are likely to catch each other's blind spots. Reflexivity is an ongoing discipline, not a problem you solve once.

commonMistakes: Agreeing that bias exists but failing to say what follows from that — sliding into 'so all interpretations are just opinions'
Denying that bias exists in archaeological interpretation
Being unable to give any specific example of bias shaping past archaeological work
followUps: Can you give me an example of an archaeological interpretation that was later revised because of bias?

subtopic: Climate and environment in the past
question: How can archaeology contribute to our understanding of past climate and environmental change?
lookingFor: Awareness of the disciplines and methods that bridge archaeology and environmental science — palynology, dendrochronology, faunal analysis — and why that matters for understanding long-term human-environment relationships.

bullets: Name specific proxy evidence types: pollen, tree rings, isotopes in bone or shell, faunal assemblages
Explain what archaeological sites can add that purely geological records cannot — human response to change
Note why this matters now — long-term baselines for climate models
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Archaeological sites preserve all kinds of environmental proxy data — pollen sequences from waterlogged deposits, animal bone that reflects species availability and hunting strategies, tree rings from preserved timbers, isotopes in shells or teeth that record seasonal temperature. What makes archaeological evidence particularly valuable is that it combines environmental data with evidence of human response: you can see not just that a climate event happened, but how communities adapted, migrated, or failed to adapt. That's something a purely geological record can't give you. It's also genuinely useful for contemporary climate science, because it extends the baseline record well beyond the instrumental period and shows the range of conditions human societies have navigated.

commonMistakes: Talking only about how climate affected people without mentioning how archaeology records environmental conditions
Not knowing any specific proxy technique by name
Treating it as purely an academic question with no relevance to the present
followUps: Can you give an example of a period where archaeology has shown how a society responded to a climate event?

question: Most professional archaeology in the UK is developer-funded. What do you think are the advantages and disadvantages of that model?
lookingFor: Awareness of how professional archaeology actually works in practice, with a balanced view of what the planning-led model achieves and where it falls short.

bullets: Explain the basic model: development triggers evaluation and excavation at the developer's expense
Name genuine advantages: vastly more sites recorded than pure research budgets could fund
Name genuine disadvantages: time pressure, publication backlogs, research questions set by development not curiosity
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: The planning-led model means that every significant development must assess and, where necessary, excavate the archaeology it would destroy, funded by the developer. The obvious advantage is scale — thousands of sites get recorded that would simply be lost if we relied on research grants alone, and it's generated huge datasets on ordinary settlement and landscape that academics have been able to draw on. The downsides are real though: there's constant time and budget pressure, which can compromise quality; publication rates for grey literature reports are poor; and the research agenda is set by where people want to build things, not by intellectual questions. I think it's the best realistic model we have, but it works much better when there's a strong research framework alongside it to make sense of what's being found.

commonMistakes: Not knowing what developer-funded archaeology means or how the planning system works
Presenting it as purely positive without the real limitations
Having no view on how the model could be improved
followUps: What would you change about the model if you could?

question: Tell us about an archaeological site that you find genuinely fascinating, and what makes it so.
lookingFor: Specific knowledge about a real site beyond the famous name — what was found, what questions it raises, why it matters — showing genuine engagement rather than just name-dropping.

bullets: Name the site and say something substantive about what it contains or what has been found there
Explain what makes it intellectually interesting, not just visually impressive
Connect it to a broader question the discipline is asking
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I keep coming back to Çatalhöyük in Turkey. It's a large Neolithic settlement occupied for about 1,200 years, and what fascinates me is how it challenges assumptions about early urban life — the houses had no streets between them, you entered through the roof, and the settlement was remarkably egalitarian in its material remains with very little evidence of hierarchy. There are also extraordinary deposits of plastered skulls and animal iconography that raise deep questions about ritual and domestic life being blurred in ways we don't have easy categories for. It's a site where the evidence keeps resisting tidy interpretation, and I find that more interesting than sites that confirm what we expected.

commonMistakes: Naming a famous site like Pompeii or Stonehenge and only saying it's impressive without any analytical comment
Showing no knowledge of what was actually found there or what it means
Picking a site they've clearly just looked up without genuine engagement
followUps: What's one open question about that site that you'd like to see resolved?

question: Looting of archaeological sites is a global problem. What do you think drives it and what, if anything, can be done about it?
lookingFor: A structured analysis of causes — poverty, demand, conflict — and a proportionate view of possible responses that goes beyond simply 'it's wrong'.

bullets: Identify demand-side and supply-side drivers — wealthy collectors and markets, poverty, conflict and state failure
Acknowledge the complexity: in some regions looting is the only economic option for local people
Suggest responses at different levels — legal frameworks, community engagement, market regulation
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: Looting has multiple causes that need to be tackled at different levels. On the supply side, poverty and state failure in conflict zones play a huge role — in Iraq and Syria, systematic looting intensified when central authority collapsed, and for many local people it was economically rational. On the demand side, wealthy collectors and a market that often turns a blind eye to provenance drive the trade. You can't solve it with enforcement alone; community engagement schemes that give local people a stake in sites can be more effective, and there's evidence they work. The harder reform is at the market end — stricter provenance requirements for auction houses and dealers, and genuine consequences for institutions that knowingly buy looted material. The 1970 UNESCO Convention is the key legal benchmark, but its implementation is patchy.

commonMistakes: Treating it as simply a law enforcement problem without the economic and political dimensions
Ignoring the demand side — wealthy buyers and auction houses
Not knowing any specific legal framework or convention
followUps: Should museums that hold objects acquired before 1970 still be held to the same standard?

question: Archaeology draws on a very wide range of disciplines — natural sciences, humanities, social sciences. Which of your existing skills do you think will serve you best, and what will you need to develop?
lookingFor: Self-aware, honest assessment of strengths and gaps, with a clear understanding of what archaeology actually demands across disciplines.

bullets: Identify a genuine existing strength and connect it concretely to what archaeology requires
Name a real gap and show you've thought about how to address it
Demonstrate awareness of the full range of skills the discipline demands
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: My strongest asset coming in is probably my comfort with quantitative work — I did statistics at A-level and I've used it for a small independent project, so the data analysis side of things, whether that's working with GIS or making sense of faunal assemblages, feels accessible to me. What I know I'll need to develop is the art-historical and classical language side — I have no Latin, which matters if I end up working on Roman Britain, and reading site reports with Latin or French sources is something I'll need to work on. I also want to get more systematic about field drawing and recording, which is a practical skill I've only touched in brief experiences.

commonMistakes: Claiming they already have all the skills they need
Naming a weakness so significant it raises doubts about the application
Vague answers about being 'curious' or 'hardworking' without connecting to specific disciplinary demands
followUps: How do you plan to start filling the gap you identified before the course begins?

question: What do you think is the most pressing challenge facing archaeology as a discipline right now?
lookingFor: A specific, substantive challenge — not a generic observation — with reasoned argument for why it matters and some sense of what addressing it would involve.

bullets: Name a specific challenge — not just 'funding' generically — with substance behind it
Argue why it is pressing relative to other challenges
Show some sense of what good progress on it would look like
idealAnswer.modelAnswer: I'd argue the biggest challenge is the publication and grey literature crisis in commercial archaeology. Hundreds of thousands of site records are produced every year through developer-funded work, but a fraction get written up in accessible, peer-reviewed form. That means we're destroying irreplaceable sites and generating data that never gets properly synthesised or used. It's not a glamorous crisis, but it means the knowledge base is growing in a deeply uneven way, and our understanding of the past is being shaped by what gets written up rather than by what was most significant. Fixing it requires sustained funding for post-excavation work and better digital infrastructure for sharing grey literature — neither of which is currently prioritised.

commonMistakes: Naming a challenge so broad it has no analytical content — 'lack of funding' or 'public interest'
Not being able to explain why the issue they name matters more than alternatives
Describing a problem with no sense of what a solution might involve
followUps: If you were advising a government heritage body, what one policy change would you recommend?
